turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: I have the opinion of the court in Case 23-621, Lackey v. Stinnie. Until recently a Virginia statute directed state courts to suspend the license of any driver who failed to pay court fines or cost that had been assessed against him for the violation of another law. The license would remain suspended until the driver paid the full amount owed or entered a court-approved payment plan. Virginia drivers whose licenses had been suspended and who asserted that they could not afford to pay the court costs or fines sued the Commissioner of the Virginia Department of Motor Vehicles. They sued under Title 42, §1983 of the United States Code which provides a cause of action for the deprivation of civil rights. Specifically, the drivers alleged that the Virginia law violated the due process and equal protection clauses. In December 2018, the District Court granted a preliminary injunction prohibiting the commission from enforcing the statute. Before Trial took place, the Virginia General Assembly repealed the law and required the permanent reinstatement of the driver's licenses. The District Court then dismissed the case as moot. Now another provision of Title 42 §1988(b) allows an Award of attorney's fees to "prevailing parties" under §1983. The drivers argued that they were entitled to attorney's fees under this section because they had prevailed at the preliminary injunction stage. The District Court declined to award fees on the ground that parties who obtained relief in court only at the preliminary injunction stage do not qualify as prevailing parties. The Fourth Circuit reversed. Today, we reversed the Fourth Circuit and hold that the drivers do not qualify as prevailing parties in court and are therefore not entitled to attorney's fees. Under what is known as the American Rule, the prevailing party is ordinarily not entitled to collect attorney's fees from the losing party. §1988(b) does not authorize us to depart from that rule in this case. We have previously recognized that the phrase "prevailing party" is a legal term of art, meaning as a leading legal dictionary puts it, the party ultimately prevailing when the matter is finally set at rest. But preliminary injunctions do not conclusively resolve legal disputes. In awarding such relief, courts assess only the likelihood of success on the merits among other factors. Preliminary injunctions are transient in nature and courts may reach a different conclusion after full consideration of the merits. Congress, if it does not like this result, can change it. In the Freedom of Information Act, for example, Congress, authorized courts to award fees when a complainant has "substantially prevailed" even if through a voluntary or unilateral change in position by the agency. Congress has not done anything like that here and we are not going to rewrite the statute as if it had. The judgment of the Court of Appeals for the Fourth Circuit is reversed and the case is remanded for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Justice Jackson has filed a dissenting opinion joined by Justice Sotomayor.
